chunks.text: We're going to talk about sort of fundamentally different ways to find big ideas or small ideas and to innovate.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: The traditional methods are based on fine to need and fill it.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: Okay.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: They are going to your target market users and saying, what do you want?

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: As you'll see, as we go, that ends up being something about incremental innovation.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: And then there are new methods based on finding, encouraging, commercializing solutions developed by users themselves.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: So, for example, in the case of the skateboard, it's not that you as a manufacturer go and interview these kids.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: Say, now children, what would you like?

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: Well, let's see, we would like to come near death.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: And so, probably what we'd like is a board that's totally uncontrolled that will allow us to go down the hill and come near death oftentimes.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: First of all, a manufacturer probably not want to do that.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: But secondly, they couldn't sort of come up with it. It wouldn't work.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: What happened instead is that the kids started building skateboards for themselves.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: The reason they're called skateboards is they took skates and they put them under boards and went sailing down the hill.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: So, the kids were well along the way. This group, in other words, lead users over here were well along the way towards innovation.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: By the time this group knew anything about it.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: And so, basically, what you'll see is the distinction is these new methods that actually go after needs and solutions at the same time.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: You didn't say to those kids, what do you think you need? You said, oh, I see your careering down the hill on a skateboard.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: Aha! And then you bring it over here where these people would say something like, I'd really like my bicycle to go a little faster.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: So, now, what I'm going to talk to you about in the next bit of this lecture is how we discovered the role of users.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: And to do that, I just have to give you some essential definitions.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: What we're talking about here, and what I'm going to do, by the way, because I know that chatting is also important.

chunks.text: There's going to be an exercise in here in a little bit where you get to say hello to your neighbor, which is always a sound strategy.

chunks.text: And then, I want you to talk about a user innovation you know something about.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: So, I'm going to tell you about user innovation. I'm going to give you some examples.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: And then, since the half-life of unused knowledge is about a nanosecond, I want you to play with it.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: And we'll do that throughout the course. You'll start to play with this stuff and sort of see whether you believe it.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: So, I know it's much more fun when you chat. Your time to chat is coming.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: So, what we're talking about, first of all, is let's take an innovation.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: Innovation's actually anything new that's put into the marketplace.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: It's different than an invention.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: An invention is a legal term that says if something meets certain standards of novelty, then that thing is an invention.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: But you could take that thing that you developed, say something wonderful that passes a standard like anti-gravity.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: You could take anti-gravity and you could throw it in the basement, assuming anti-gravity goes down.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: You could throw it in the basement and store it. Never put it into the marketplace. It does not become an innovation.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: It's only an innovation when it's used.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: An innovation can be something trivial or non-trivial.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: It can be anything from anti-gravity that's used to a new pop-top on a can.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: But then it's an innovation.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: Now, we're talking about the functional source of innovation.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: An innovation is a user innovation when the developer expects to benefit from using it.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: Now, this is not just you as an individual.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: This is you also as a firm. So, take Boeing, for example.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: Boeing is a user innovator when it develops, say, a new machine to build airplanes.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: Because it's trying to use it.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: Boeing, that same firm is a manufacturer innovator when it develops a new airplane to sell.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: So, you, when you try to develop something to protect you from your brother when you're growing up or your sister,

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: and it's just not something on the market that works.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: You just cannot buy the proper brother's scaring device.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: So, you have to develop your own.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: Again, you're a user innovator.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: You're not planning to sell that to all the neighbor kids.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: You are actually doing that for yourself.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: So, whether you're an individual or a firm,

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: if your reason is to use it, then you're a user.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: If your reason is to benefit from selling it, you're a manufacturer.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: Now, I'm going to give you three slides here showing how we do this.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: How do we find out where the innovations come from?

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: Now, you remember that I told you that the standard model is find a need and fill it.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: That is the manufacturer goes and takes a look to see what average users want.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: It makes sense. They're the target market.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: And then comes back and innovates.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: So, what we wanted to do was to see whether this was true.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: Now, this happens to be scientific instruments, but what we actually did was this over a bunch of things,

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: you know, like sports equipment and so on and so forth.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: The point here is that what we do is we take these product lines.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: You see, if you're a manufacturer, you have a product line, right?

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: These represent product lines for your company.

chunks.text: So, what would you like to know?

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: You'd like to know where the major innovations come from over time.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: If there's a pattern in that, then you can do it again.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: If you mistake what the pattern is, then in fact, you're going about it the wrong way.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: And you'll see that this is the case.

chunks.title: Lecture3.mp3
chunks.text: So, find a need and fill it would say these major improvements consist of us going out there to the field
